title: What China Thinks It Can Gain From a Disordered World
author: Oriana Skylar Mastro
text: Podcast What China Thinks It Can Gain From a Disordered WorldA Conversation With Oriana Skylar Mastro Published on July 9, 2026 Play Listen to the Episode 63 minutes 00:00 / 1:03:22 0.5×1×1.5×2× MP3 Close audio player Print Print this article Save Sign in and save to read later Close Share What China Thinks It Can Gain From a Disordered World A Conversation With Oriana Skylar Mastro Share in email Share on Facebook Share on X Share on LinkedIn Article link: https://www.foreignaffairs.com/podcasts/what-china-thinks-it-can-gain-disordered-worldhttps://www.foreignaffairs.com/podcasts/what-china-thinks-it-can-gain-disordered-world Request reprint permissions here.Episode Details Like the rest of us, China’s leaders are confronting a world in turmoil. They see uncertain American commitments to allies, even as many of those allies ramp up their own defense efforts. They see changing relationships, whether deepening ties between Russia and North Korea or new connections among anxious middle powers. They see American military action in the Middle East and Western Hemisphere, with effects on American power in China’s own region.And yet, as Oriana Skylar Mastro sees it, far from surprising Beijing, such disruptions are being taken as confirmation of what Chinese leader Xi Jinping often refers to as “great change unseen in a century.” Mastro has written a series of essays for Foreign Affairs on how Beijing aims to shape and exploit that change. She joined Dan Kurtz-Phelan to discuss China’s strategy for navigating this turmoil—and what the United States should, and should not, do in response.Sources:“Kim’s Dangerous Liaisons” by Oriana Skylar Mastro“China’s Agents of Chaos” by Oriana Skylar Mastro“The Next Tripartite Pact” by Oriana Skylar Mastro“The Taiwan Temptation” by Oriana Skylar Mastro“The Stealth Superpower” by Oriana Skylar Mastro“North Korea as It Is” by Victor ChaThis episode of The Foreign Affairs Interview was produced by Rose Kohler and Kanishk Tharoor, with audio engineering by Christopher Koch and Markus Zakaria and original music by Robin Hilton. Special thanks to Irina Hogan.This is a rough transcript of the conversation. Please consult the underlying audio prior to citation.DAN KURTZ-PHELANOriana, thank you so much for doing this.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROYeah, thank you for having me.DAN KURTZ-PHELANYour most recent piece for Foreign Affairs is on the intersection of the China, Russia, and North Korea challenges for U.S. foreign policy. But you’ve done fantastic essays over the years for the magazine on Chinese strategy more broadly, on China-Russia, on China–North Korea, on threats to Taiwan. So I want to start a little bit more broadly than that most recent piece with your read of Chinese strategy more generally at this moment in history. The starting point for that seems to be the question of how China understands the second term of Trump’s presidency and whether that represents a rupture or something else. What is your understanding of the Chinese understanding of this moment, how it sees this moment in history?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROWell, I think with Trump in office also with all the ongoing conflicts with Iran, with Venezuela, with Ukraine now the past couple of years, there’s a tendency to think that everything has completely changed. Emerging technology where we are with AI, people like to say, well, now the whole international system, everything’s different. The character, nature of warfare, everything’s different. So China must be just completely rethinking its strategy. But the bottom line is that China’s strategy to become a great power was somewhat resistant to a lot of these changes because it predicted that most of these things would be occurring.It’s not like in the 1990s when the Chinese decided that they wanted to leverage their economic power to become a military power, which I wrote about in Foreign Affairs, “The Stealth Superpower,” that they didn’t predict that, for example, there’d be volatility in the U.S. system. They actually debated for many years how the United States would react once it recognized it was a declining power. Now we might see the U.S. position differently, but the Chinese have a series of metrics to understand where they are vis-à-vis U.S. power. And they decided that the United States was going to become much more aggressive towards them when their economy was two-thirds the size of China’s. And that happened in the first Trump administration.And then they predicted that the United States would become much more risk acceptant in terms of use of force during this period of time. So these wars in Iran, Venezuela, they’re just confirming the things that the Chinese already knew. And then of course to be a tech superpower, emerging tech, I mean, Xi Jinping has had these science and technology plans that put that at the forefront of their strategy for a long time.So it might change a little bit around the edges like, okay, now they’re going to be a little bit nicer. They’re going to be a little bit more difficult. They’re going to have a summit. They’re not going to talk to us in terms of the tactics of the strategy. But the overall approach of trying to strengthen China at home to become more self-sufficient so it’s not so reliant on the United States and its allies and partners to pursue its own vision of the international system, that’s been the same for 30 years.DAN KURTZ-PHELANDo you think that China’s net assessment, if you can put it that way, is correct? I mean, I know the statistic you noted on the size of the economy is purchasing power parity. People here often use nominal GDP comparisons. How do you assess the Chinese net assessment?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo they did sort of recalibrate a couple of years ago when they were very optimistic they were going to overtake the United States in power. And then they recalibrated and they said, listen, the United States and China are both going to be great powers on a different plane than everyone else, but we’re never going to surpass the United States. We’re going to grow in parallel. And as I’ve written, the Chinese from a far weaker resource position historically have been able to compete with the United States. So it’s not necessarily a game changer that they “don’t overtake” the United States. So I think that reassessment was important, but there is a lot of confidence there in Beijing. Now you go to Washington, you go to other places, what, for ten years we’ve talked about demographics, we’ve talked about the overreach of the Chinese government, of the output of the economy, but at least from their perspective, these are problems that they can manage over time.So I think when it comes to assessing their own role in the world, they are correct in their assessments of power. Sometimes where they misstep is the assessment of the causes of current challenges. And so their assessment of the international environment for themselves is that it’s very difficult. They face a negative security environment in East Asia vis-à-vis their neighbors like Japan, Australia, the Philippines, but they think that it’s the fault of the United States that they have these security issues, not that they’re behaving in any way that is causing the threat perceptions to grow. So the problem is if they would assess that they were the problem, they might recalibrate their strategy. But their view is like, listen, before we were really nice to everyone and people still talked about us as a problem. Now nobody likes us, but at least they do what we tell them to do. And I think they think that’s working for them.DAN KURTZ-PHELANAnd does that mean Trump and his use of force, his approach to alliances, his approach to international institutions, everything else just doesn’t change that much in the Chinese view? Is that your view of it?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo on the margins, in their view, maybe this hastens the decline of the United States, but the mechanisms through which that decline was going to happen weren’t particularly clear. So the fact that you have now a populist leader that comes into power, rejects the international system, puts huge strain on the alliance structure that allows the United States to be a global military power. It’s not that the details of that were something that were predictable, mostly because Biden was doing the complete opposite. The Chinese were very concerned about the coalition-building U.S. force posture in the Indo-Pacific had strengthened significantly under President Biden. So I don’t mean to suggest that they have some sort of crystal ball and they’re reading all of those things in terms of exactly how it’s happening. But the bottom line is that the United States tendency to engage in foreign military interventions of which President Trump is not the first president to be using force abroad.That historically has been something in Chinese strategic thinking that is going to perpetuate the U.S. decline, that it’s extremely costly in the Chinese view. It does not achieve the U.S. objectives hardly ever again in the Chinese view. And so because of that, now we’ve sort of accelerated, it does tell them that we are in a dangerous period. So people often ask me, are the Chinese more deterred or less deterred by the United States under this administration? And I would say it’s kind of a mixed bag because on one hand our capabilities are weakened. Our posture in the Indo-Pacific, the pages of Foreign Affairs have written about the lack of munitions, lack of readiness, lack of focus to strengthen our posture there. Allies and partners, we can’t get the access basing and overflight we need to execute operations. That makes China very confident.On the other hand, you look at the resolve side and they’re worried that we’re becoming more risk acceptant, that we’re like, listen, yes, this might be costly. Yes, we might lose, but we’re doing it anyway. And that can be a very point of caution for the Chinese not to get in our crosshairs. And that’s why I think Xi Jinping was very happy about having a summit with President Trump to be like, listen, focus your attention somewhere else and let us do our thing.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWhat does that mean when it comes to the risk of Chinese use of force in the Taiwan Strait? And I would note that you wrote a piece in early 2021 before this was one of the top tier issues in American national security debates about the risk that China might do something precipitous in Taiwan. How do you assess that risk now and how has it changed under Trump the second time around?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo I don’t know, Dan, if I ever told you this story. I always tell this story of that Foreign Affairs piece because originally, I don’t know if you remember, you asked me to write about something different and I said, “Hey, listen, I want to write this piece about U.S. strategy and how it should change given the fact that China’s likely to use force over Taiwan.” And you said to me, “What are you talking about? China’s not going to use force against Taiwan.” I said, “What are you talking about? Of course they are.”And my biggest concern about writing that piece, which as you know, led to this huge debate, numbers of people wrote response essays. My hugest concern was not that it was going to be controversial. My hugest concern is that my colleagues would judge me for writing something that everyone knew. That was the most basic idea just to get in the prestigious pages of Foreign Affairs.So when I wrote that, I thought it was like a nothing burger. And then the response was so intense. It really shows, I think the Taiwan issue is really the divide in the China community. So where am I on this issue? As I wrote then, I still believe that the number one factor that is determining this Taiwan contingency, when China is going to use force, whether China uses force, is the assessment of their own capabilities. The United States for a long time now has not been deterring China over Taiwan. It’s no longer like we threaten to intervene. And they’re like, oh, well, now that the United States threatened us to intervene, we can’t do this. Because there’s different types of contingencies, but the only one China’s preparing for is this fait accompli quick seizure, two and a half weeks, three weeks. If they can’t do that, they’re not going to do it short of something crazy like Taiwan declaring independence.And the United States can do very little in that short period of time. And that was the right strategy to try to strengthen Taiwan’s capabilities, not for self-defense. I hate that term. Taiwan has zero chance of defending itself. Let’s all be clear. People are like, “Well, Ukraine did this against Russia.” It’s like I go up for a run and someone’s like, “Well, this Olympic runner could run a four-minute mile.” And you’re like, “Great, that is information irrelevant to this situation.” Taiwan cannot defend itself, but it can hold out long enough for the United States to get there. So if we get to that point, that would impose caution on China. Now what has changed? Xi Jinping told the military to be ready by 2027. The biggest issues they had were in command and control and logistics. They got the equipment, they got the numbers of the equipment.They’ve been reforming the process. They do have problems recruiting college level personnel, but they’ve been doing more enhanced training. Xi Jinping looked at his troops, did what Putin should have done and said, “You have a lot of stuff. I don’t think you know how to use it.” There’s good information flow to Xi. All of that’s in place. The command and control, Xi threw out the vast majority of the top leaders of the PLA over the past year. So we don’t have people right now in place to be able to execute this campaign. So I do think 2027 was never a deadline. It was always a no earlier than date, but I think they’re not going to have the command and control ready in time. And so it pushes it to 2028. So I don’t think it’s the case that there’s a window of opportunity. Everyone’s like, Trump’s going to sell out Taiwan.Trump’s not going to do anything with Taiwan. Again, it’s not really about U.S. intervention at this point and they can never be sure. If they’re not ready to go, they’re not going to go. And in their mind, it’s not like Trump leaves, someone else comes in and all of a sudden the United States has solved all of its problems. So they still have time to figure it out. But once that military is ready to go, in my mind, I continue to say there is no reason. It’s very tempting. That’s why that piece was called “The Taiwan Temptation.” If they can do it, they’ve waited 75 years. Why would they wait? And when I talk to people in Beijing, they say the exact same thing. So I don’t think that mentality has changed much.DAN KURTZ-PHELANIf I remember my surprise at your point several years ago, and then the debate, at least part of the debate that ensued in our pages and elsewhere after the piece was published, it was not just about the risk of aggressive Chinese action toward Taiwan, but also the kind of military operation that you foresaw. A lot of the response argued that it would be gray zone strategies or a blockade or something like that. You’re still skeptical that that’s how China would do it.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROYeah, I mean, I think those things are going to happen, but I don’t see them as reunification campaigns. I think China gets upset and they’re like, “We’re going to punish you for doing this thing, and so we’re going to do a two-week blockade.” But it’s not going to be a reunification campaign because the blockade gives the United States the time to amass forces in the region so that if we do decide to fully support Taiwan, then the landing becomes very difficult. So it’s kind of a very risky strategy. So all that gray zone stuff I think is prepping the battle space. It is weakening the Taiwan military, weakening the resolve of the military. But I am assuming, and I think a lot of polling and other research supports this, but just to be clear about my assumption, I am assuming that the people of Taiwan are not going to fully capitulate Communist Party rule unless there is some use of force.And I think the polls demonstrate that, that they’re not ready to come to the table and say, “Oh, now that you’ve done a lot of exercises, now that there’s these misinformation campaigns, we would like to get rid of our government and our military and have you govern the island.” So that’s why I think it’s highly unlikely that the Chinese are able to do this without use of force. It’s very different from Hong Kong. People often are like, “Well, they did this in Hong Kong.” They controlled Hong Kong at the time. They had infiltrated, penetrated. They militarily, physically, everything controlled Hong Kong so they had a lot of different levers to use in that scenario that they don’t have in Taiwan.DAN KURTZ-PHELANEven if Beijing does not watch the war in Ukraine or the war in Iran and imagine itself as Russia in that scenario or as the United States, what is it learning about the changing nature of warfare as it watches those conflicts?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROWell, I think there’s a lot of takeaways that they are getting from the recent wars. China had previously thought it was very important to have things like good intelligence, good command and control, but these wars just really highlight even more so that if you’re going to do some joint operations, these require intelligence, air dominance. It requires that asymmetry against the United States that Ukraine had against Russia and that Iran has against the United States. So trying to focus on smaller, less expensive platforms that you can mass produce. And so you can bring that mass of firepower in a more effective way and in a cheaper way against your adversary. Because in a war of attrition, it’s not even so much which side is losing more people. It’s also which side is losing their capability in terms of platforms. So I think that is definitely a lesson that has been learned.Now, there’s some lessons that we’re hoping the Chinese take away from these conflicts that they might not necessarily see the same parallels. Like with Ukraine, many people are hoping they take away the fact that wars may not end quickly. Because the contingency of Taiwan, the only one they’re considering is one in which they can do it in three weeks. But it didn’t end quickly in Ukraine because of industrial capacity issues, ammunition issues and logistics. And for the Chinese, the only one they’re currently struggling with is still logistics. The unmanned systems, cheap drones, decoys, long-range weapons are still things that they’re very focused on. We’re hoping that they learn from Venezuela that it’s not easy to just remove a top leader and put in a whole new political system.But again, that situation’s very different from Taiwan where China has been engaged in a lot of disinformation campaigns, has basically to a higher degree, penetrated that political system in Taiwan. So they might have more confidence that they could do something with Taiwan like they did in Hong Kong in which they have a governor or a leader of their own that they put into place. But those are some less that we’re hoping they take away from these conflicts. But in the end, things like you need a lot of munitions, you need to be able to mass produce them, and you want capabilities that are cheap and effective at bringing masks. These are things the Chinese knew before. And even if they’re more encouraged along those lines, they are farther ahead than the United States in perfecting that way of war.DAN KURTZ-PHELANTo your mind, has anything that Donald Trump has done, especially on or since his visit to China in May, changed China’s calculus here? He has held up the most recent arms package in Taiwan. He’s repeated framing that he presumably heard from Xi Jinping when they were meeting. Does that affect this calculus at all?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROI think it does affect the calculus in the short term. It’s definitely a madman’s theory in that Beijing finds Donald Trump really hard to predict in terms of what he’s going to do. They don’t find him particularly rational in his decision-making. And so if we go back to the theory of victory over Taiwan, that theory of victory is based on assumptions about what a rational U.S. president would do. So the two and a half to three weeks, the scenario is such that China takes Taiwan. And the question is, what is the United States really going to do at that point? If Taiwan has capitulated, if the United States hasn’t engaged, are we then going to launch a full-fledged war against the United States? That’s an assumption of U.S. rationality of costs and benefits. Who knows? Maybe Donald Trump feels like Xi Jinping made him look bad and Donald Trump has said that, that Xi Jinping wouldn’t be so stupid to do that.It’s also the case that in that two and a half to three weeks in which I’ve said the United States can’t really do much to defend Taiwan, that is also based on a rational calculation. Because of course the United States can respond immediately. A U.S. president can decide to deploy our forces in Japan, in the Philippines immediately to defend Taiwan. The problem is they’re not survivable. So that’s a nice way of saying that everybody would die because we don’t have enough of them.So the assumption in Beijing, of course, is that a U.S. president wouldn’t do that. But given that they’re uncertain about how Donald Trump even makes decisions, let alone where those decisions come from, I do think they impose caution in Beijing short of being pushed into a war by a full-on declaration of independence or something by President Lai, I find it highly unlikely coupled with the fact that they’re not ready yet, that China would use force against Taiwan during the Trump administration.DAN KURTZ-PHELANSo if some of Trump’s hard to comprehend decisions cut against Taiwanese interests, the uncertainty more than outweighs that to your mind?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROYes. I don’t think the Chinese are like, “You know what? I think Donald Trump is going to sell out Taiwan and I’m putting all my eggs in that basket.” I mean, I don’t have a sense that they feel confident in their assessments of President Trump.DAN KURTZ-PHELANI suppose it’s a separate question, what it does to resolve on Taiwan, but that’s a different issue.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROBoth to the Chinese and to those on Taiwan, I’m trying to enhance deterrence on both sides of the strait. And at least what I like to say in a more optimistic view is that the United States policy has remained the same, and that is to maintain the status quo across the strait. Now throughout history, sometimes the United States pressures more the China side of the strait. Sometimes we pressure more the Taiwan side of strait. You could argue during the last Bush administration, we put a lot more pressure on Taiwan to tone down its activity than we put on Beijing. So it kind of goes back and forth on who we pressure. And right now maybe we’re pressuring Taiwan more than many people feel comfortable with. But I think the bottom line policy of the United States to maintain the status quo across the strait has remained the same.DAN KURTZ-PHELANThe other thing that’s been striking in watching China both over the last several months since the start of the most recent war in Iran and then also watching China’s policy towards Ukraine over the years is it’s slightly ambivalent, to use a word I think you used in an essay in Foreign Affairs in 2024, attitude towards some of these partners that seem to be in this alignment of upheaval or autocracy or whatever term you want to apply to it. You argue in that 2024 piece that China, while committed to its partnerships with Russia and to some degree North Korea, does see some downsides and costs to those. It’s not a straightforward alliance commitment in the way that the United States would’ve made before Donald Trump, I suppose. What is your reading of China’s response to those wars when it comes to their partners?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo there’s a few things that have changed, but first I just want to highlight what has not. And that is that China does not have traditional alliances like the United States. And there’s many reasons for this that I also have discussed in Foreign Affairs, but largely because they feel like it’s much more effective if they have good relations with everybody. There’s an othering that happens. If you are fully behind Iran, you’re going to have problems with Israel. If you’re fully behind North Korea, you’re going to have problems with South Korea. Russia and the European Union, the European Union, which is China’s largest trading partner. So far they’ve done a pretty good job of hanging out with the Indians, hanging out with the Pakistanis, not choosing sides. And that has worked for them and they feel like that’s the way to move forward. The problem with these conflicts I think, and the source of change stems less from China and more from their partners because it has become painfully obvious that China has no ability and no desire to protect its partners from military intervention.The Chinese leadership had just met with President Maduro right before he was taken by U.S. forces. The Chinese have a very close relationship with Iran. It did not matter when the United States was making decisions about whether or not to use force against these countries. So one of the things that I’m looking for in terms of something that could drastically change in terms of Chinese strategy and then drastically change the international system is whether or not they start to feel that they do need the ability to project power beyond the region. Because to date, we’ve been talking about Chinese overseas basings for decades, but it has not come to fruition because there’s a lot of reasons China does not want to project military power far beyond its borders. It relies on political and economic power. But if partners say, listen, China, being able to trade with you is not enough anymore. We need some source of protection and the Chinese feel compelled to provide that protection, then we might see a more active military abroad.Another aspect of this is that China has relied mainly on police training, law enforcement cooperation with countries to protect its interests overseas. So the way it assessed threats to its interests in other countries was that that threat was going to come from within. The threat to their economic interests in Venezuela comes from instability in Venezuela. Now all of a sudden the threat comes from outside and the mechanisms that they have been using to protect their interests are no longer valid. And so I have heard whisperings in Beijing. It’s not enough yet for me to send you an email saying, “Okay, we need a Foreign Affairs article on this.” But I hear whisperings that there is some discussion of, okay, we need to have some sort of deterrent and options.And I do think Russia is part of that. They think their relationship with Russia maybe enhances that deterrent against the United States for military intervention because Russia has those similar relationships and Russia could potentially intervene, but China doesn’t have control over that tool, so it’s not ideal.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWhat is the Chinese theory of the Russia relationship and its value to Chinese strategy over the long term? There’s been obviously lots of attention paid to the Xi-Putin relationship and the so-called no-limits partnership. Every time Putin visits Beijing, there’s lots of energy put into reading the tea leaves and statements and body language and everything that comes out. On the other hand, when you look at the Europe-China relationship, the trade relationship, it does seem like China is paying some real costs with its largest trading partner, as you pointed out, for its ongoing support of Russia. I’m curious how you think about that in China’s strategy over the long term.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROWell, it’s interesting because I would’ve read it a bit differently. And as you know, I just got back from a year in Europe and even though I speak three European languages, so I kind of can pretend that I’m much more open to the European ideas. Once I open my mouth, everyone’s like, “Wow, you are really American about these things.” But one thing I say a lot in Europe, which is really unpopular, if China felt like they had to choose between Europe and Russia, they would choose Europe every single time. Every single time. It is so much more important to them, the economic relationship with Europe than what they get from Russia. Now, maybe historically, largely what they got from Russia is the sort of technology equipment and know-how to modernize their military. And you can look at aspects of their surface fleet, aspects of their fighter force, bomber force, and you can clearly point those to Russia.There’s still areas where they could improve a lot thanks to the cooperation in particular and undersea warfare if the Russians were more forthcoming about quieting technology, also in early warning systems. So there’s still areas where cooperation could happen, but a lot of that has already happened.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWhat about on China’s pretty significant nuclear buildup? Is Russia an important part of that or has it been in the past?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo I don’t think that Russia is a part of that from the Chinese perspective, and it is a part of it in terms of how the United States sees the buildup, but in a way that’s very negative for China. Because in the United States, there’s a lot of discussion about the three-body problem, meaning that some people feel like the United States needs to have more nuclear weapons than China and Russia combined given the nature of their military alignment, which would lead to a massive nuclear buildup in the United States, which makes China less comfortable about its deterrent. And so that’s sort of a bad news story from the China perspective of what their relationship with Russia might do.And so if the Europeans actually had some backbone to stand up to China in any significant way economically and said, listen, if you keep on supporting Russia and Ukraine, we’re not going to trade this with you and not going to trade that with you, that would really put China in a difficult position to make some decisions.I’m working on a book right now about China and Russia. And one of the things that you can see historically for both sides is the number one thing that pulls these countries apart is when they feel like the behavior of their partner is worsening their security situation. And so you look back in the ’50s, it’s like the Soviet Union wants to taunt with the United States and Mao Zedong is shelling the offshore islands of Taiwan. And they’re like, “You’re making this a lot more difficult for me to manage the United States.” And it’s okay for both to feel like the United States is the number one adversary. That has happened historically, but if they feel like they have different ways of dealing with the United States, that could pull them apart.So if China came to that realization, I think they would tone down the relationship with Russia. It’s just that the way I see it, they haven’t really paid any significant costs in terms of the closeness that they have with Putin and with Russia.DAN KURTZ-PHELANAnd how expansive are the upsides from a European perspective there and from an American perspective? Can you imagine China putting pressure on Russia to end the war, taking an active role in that, cutting off some of the dual-use technologies that have been fairly important to allowing Russia to continuing the war effort?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo on one hand, I’ve heard people say, “Oh, China really benefits from this war in Ukraine, and they want it to go on and on and on.” I don’t really see any indication of that. I don’t think the Chinese are happy about the war in Ukraine. I don’t think they want to perpetuate the war in Ukraine. But to your question, you’re kind of already alluding to it, they also don’t want to put a lot of skin in the game. They love mediation diplomacy. So they love to be like, “Hey guys, we’re here. We’ll host you in Beijing. We have a plan.” They see it gets them a lot of political power to be the one to bring countries to the table, but they’re not going to stick their neck out. Just the same way with the North Koreans. They would love it if the North Koreans gave up nuclear weapons, but they’re not going to stick their neck out because in the end they see that that kind of resolution would largely benefit the United States and they would be the ones paying the cost for the resolution, which they don’t want.So I think they have been in the past different degrees of encouraging to the Russians to stop the war. But what I have talked to Chinese military officers, high level officers, and I’ve asked them about this, I’ve said, “Listen, can you do something about Putin? Can you do something about Ukraine?” They say to me, “We can’t even control Kim Jong Un. You think we can control Vladimir Putin? You’re insane.” So I think they’re also trying to balance those things out.DAN KURTZ-PHELANDo you see policy options for the U.S. when it comes to fanning tensions, encouraging that divergence over maybe not the next year, but over the next ten years?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROI think there are opportunities there, but in terms of strategy of dealing with China, this is obviously an oversimplification, but there’s always the, do we make things worse for China versus do we make things better for the United States? President Biden, for example, is very focused on enhancing U.S. power and U.S. resilience, while I would argue that President Trump in the first administration was focused on undercutting Chinese power. So you get the same thing, which is a big difference in power, but you have different strategies of doing it.I think it’s the same with this alliance framework. You can try to create tensions between the two. And I think one way of doing that is mainly the economic pressure from the Europeans. But there’s also, if you want to sort of counterbalance what is happening there, the clearest way of doing that is to have better relationships between the United States and its allies and partners, which is not the direction that this administration is going.DAN KURTZ-PHELANI don’t mean to pin you down on a prediction here, but it would be your bet that ten years from now, all of the hyperventilation about the no limits, China-Russia partnership and whatever Russian vassalage to China will seem a little bit overheated?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo I think that’s the wrong way of looking at it. I mean, I think it is overheated in that these countries are not going to fight together. There’s no mutual defense treaty. But I argue that that’s because great powers do not form military alliances in that way. It’s not useful for us to compare it to the United States and Japan or the United States and the UK, because great powers tend to have nonaggression packs. What they want from the other great power is please don’t do anything while I’m off fighting. And they also want help building up their own militaries. And so China is better prepared to fight the United States because of its relationship with Russia. It’s more the impact during peacetime, I would argue, than whatever Russia would do in wartime.And we see from Ukraine even the smallest amount of support. In this case, China continued provision of dual-use technologies and continued trade and buying of oil from Russia. Even the smallest amount of support can help that country prolong a conflict for a very long period of time. So on one hand, I think it is overblown how “close” we think these two countries are, but it’s completely understated the impact that just a little bit of friendship between these countries can have on the nature of a hypothetical war.DAN KURTZ-PHELANAs the Chinese have watched the growing relationship between the North Koreans and Putin, especially since North Korea, I think to many people’s surprise, entered the war in 2024, entered the war in Ukraine. How has China understood that and how does it see it affecting both of those relationships, its relation with Russia and with North Korea?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo China is not thrilled about the closeness between Russia and North Korea for a lot of reasons, primarily because they feel like there’s a lack of control that they have over North Korea the closer North Korea gets to Russia. They also see this discussion of the autocratic access, the autocratic alignment, the autocratic quad of countries like the United States, but also allies and partners in the region like Japan, Australia looking at these dynamics and kind of getting really worried and panicking and saying, “Wow, it really looks like these countries are coming together. We also need to come together.”So the Chinese have tried to at least rhetorically and in their actions say, “We have no part of that. Russia and North Korea, these are sovereign entities so they can do what they want to do.” And at least when I talk to Chinese government officials, they’ll say, “We understand why they’re doing it given the nature of their threat environments,” but the Chinese don’t want to be seen as supporting it. They don’t feel like they’re going to stop it. And I think one of my first Foreign Affairs pieces, like ten years ago, I argued that Russia would not play a significant role in a North Korean contingency because the Chinese don’t want them there and so the Chinese would make sure that they stayed out.I don’t think that’s true anymore. I think now in a Korea contingency, the Chinese would not really be in a position given the nature of the mutual defense agreement between the Russians and the North Koreans to say to Russia, we don’t want you fighting in this war. So it definitely complicates the situation for China in terms of managing the Korean peninsula and then also managing the U.S. allies in the region that might strengthen the connectivity between themselves because of what they see happening in the region.DAN KURTZ-PHELANHow much has Russian support for North Korea in return for both North Korean munitions and troops that have gone to fight against the Ukrainians, how much has that Russian support enhanced both North Korean capabilities, nuclear or conventional? And from China’s perspective especially, how much has that increased the risk of some kind of crisis on the Korean Peninsula?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo I think it has significantly increased the likelihood of crisis on the Korean Peninsula through two mechanisms. One which you alluded to, which is that the North Koreans, their military capabilities have been improving thanks to the relationship with the Russians in two ways. One, you have North Korean troops that are actually engaged in combat with Russia in the war in Ukraine. And initially you can look at some metrics. Initially they were doing very poorly. You had higher casualties. We can see now that they’re getting this on-the-ground experience and that they are getting better. I talked to a very senior U.S. Army officer, a three-star about this in which they said that in many cases they feel like North Korean troops now have more real-world combined armed experience that is relevant to the Korean contingency than the U.S. Army does. So I don’t want us to dismiss that kind of experience.And then those individuals are now going back to North Korea and they’re training forces in North Korea. Then of course you do have the equipment and the technology transfer aspect of it. North Korea that sent thousands of troops to fight, they didn’t do this out of the kindness of their own heart. There was a deal made in which North Korea not only gets this live operational environment where it can test its own weapons and its equipment, but that they also get some secured technology transfers in exchange. Advanced satellite technology from Russia, for example, would really help the North Koreans have better surveillance and targeting data.So as they become closer and closer together, the conventional capabilities of North Korea becomes more significant, increasing their confidence that they could execute some sort of limited offense against South Korea. And then the second thing has to do with that dynamic with China, which is as much as we’ve been complaining about China and its relationship with North Korea, and maybe rightfully so, the Chinese have been a constraining force for many years.They’ve voted with the United States in the UN to put pressure on the North Koreans to give up their nuclear weapons. They have at times also supported sanctions against North Korea to pressure them to behave themselves. And now it’s really a lot harder for China to play the bad guy because they’re concerned about losing their influence with North Korea. North Korea now has an alternative in the Russians. So this sole dependence on China that existed before with the North Koreans that constrained Kim’s decision-making in which the North Koreans relied on Beijing for the vast majority of energy, food, trade, diplomatic protection, that has now been weakened.And we do see that the Chinese have now voted with the Russians in support of North Korea and the UN instead of pressuring them in recent votes. So I think the North Koreans are a little bit more unleashed, they’re a little bit more confident, and that might lead to some more aggressive behavior in the future.DAN KURTZ-PHELANAnd how have you read the shift in Chinese policy towards North Korea in response to that growing closeness between Kim and Putin? Do you see it as a meaningful change? They’ve obviously talked a little bit less about denuclearization. Xi Jinping made his first visit to Pyongyang not long ago in several years. Does that represent a meaningful change in Chinese strategy or they’re just trying to be a little bit more accommodating to make sure it maintains those ties in the face of that closeness?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROI don’t think it’s a fundamental change in the overall objective of Chinese strategy, which is just make sure nothing happens of anything significance on the Korean Peninsula. But it is significant in terms of the approach and the tactics. I mean, Xi Jinping, it was so predictable and this happened I think right after that Foreign Affairs piece on this relationship in which I said the Chinese are going to try to improve their relationship with the North Koreans. And I had a series of articles the first time Xi went to visit with Kim in which they had never met before. But when President Trump was willing to talk to Kim, again, the Chinese were like, “Whoa, whoa, whoa, we’re being cut out of this.” And immediately Xi said, “Let me go meet with Kim.” So it was so predictable that the Chinese were going to then say, okay, now that they felt like they were losing that upper hand with the North Koreans, they were going to do a high-level visit to restate the relationship.And so yeah, I think the tactic, it’s the same thing in terms of what they’re trying to achieve, but now that includes a much warmer approach to the North Koreans than existed previously.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWhat do you think the U.S. response should be? There was a piece in the same issue that your most recent one appeared in by Victor Cha arguing that it was time for the U.S. to give up on denuclearization and work to what he called the cold peace with North Korea in part to try to peel it off a bit from Moscow in Beijing. Do you see alternative approaches? Do you think that’s roughly the right way of going about those?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROI do. And it’s the same with ending the war in Ukraine. It’s like we have to be pragmatic about these things. There’s no situation that I see that we move forward with the North Koreans in which there isn’t going to be some sort of agreement about at least initially end of war agreement and then trying to more contain the problem than make it go away in terms of denuclearization.With Ukraine, I just don’t see a future in which the Russians stop fighting if they have to give up all the territory they gain through aggression. We don’t like “rewarding” bad behavior. People will argue, and I’m sympathetic to this, that will encourage other countries to behave similarly if they feel like there’s benefits to doing so. But you could also look at it from a different perspective and say, was it really so great for the North Koreans to develop these nuclear weapons?I mean, yes, after 30 years of tensions and conflicts and threats of war and trying to starve them out, we agreed to stop trying to do that. But that’s also not a great pathway that another country would be like, you know what? That’s what I want to do. So I’m not convinced that another country would be encouraged by what has occurred for Russia and Ukraine and what has occurred for North Korean denuclearization, even if in the end they don’t lose everything for going down that path. So I think we do have to be more pragmatic about these issues or else they will continue to be security challenges for another three decades.DAN KURTZ-PHELANAs you look at the prospect of a more assertive or aggressive or risk-taking Kim in North Korea or changing Chinese policy, whether in the Taiwan Strait or South China Sea or elsewhere, how do you assess the state of American deterrence? You noted the problem of weapons and troops and everything else being shifted to the Middle East and just the fact the U.S. military not having what it needs in the region. There’s been obviously a lot of U.S. policy and rhetoric over the last year and a half that has cast some doubt on some of the alliance commitments that the U.S. has in Asia. There’s talk about more limited U.S.–South Korea military relationship. What does all that mean for deterrence in the face of some of these growing threats?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo in the national security strategy, Korea wasn’t mentioned. In the defense strategy, I think it was mentioned just in the context of South Korea has to spend more on its military. So it’s definitely the case that unlike the first Trump administration, which I would argue the Korean Peninsula was the number one priority in the initial years that the administration is not a priority now. But I don’t think that U.S. deterrence in the Korean Peninsula situation has been significantly weakened because the nature of that war is just fundamentally different than a Taiwan contingency that you’re projecting power over vast distances. It’s mainly an air and sea battle. China’s fighting from its own territory. The United States is fighting from thousands and thousands of miles away.And the Korean Peninsula, for the most part, the conventional capability is going to be brought to bear. The ground forces are the South Koreans that are right there, that are numerous and resolved and highly trained to do so. Taiwan just increased its conscription from three months to a year. The South Koreans have had conscription for time immemorial. So they’re more ready.And then of course, unlike a Taiwan situation, which you have no U.S. capability on the island of Taiwan, you have tens of thousands of U.S. troops in Korea. So the situation with a Korean war is not who would win. I mean, in any major exercise or war game I’ve been a part of, the United States and South Korea always win and they always win quickly. We’re talking seven to ten days. The problem with the Korea situation is how many people are dead by the end of it.What can North Korea do to make that victory as painful as possible? And not only with the artillery, which I do think is overstated because people count pieces of artillery and not launchers. It’s like if you said, oh, this person has one gun, but a thousand bullets, they can kill a thousand people. You’re like, well, it kind of takes some time to reload and gives them some time to take away those launchers. So I think it’s a bit overblown, the artillery threat, but North Korea has nuclear weapons that they could use. And that’s why I’ve written that I think the Chinese that have a plan to secure 85 percent of North Korean nuclear weapons if they got involved in the conflict in the first few days, we should not be deterring Chinese involvement in that war, even if it means less control over an end of war solution.So from that perspective, the deterrent for the United States and South Korea is really a cost imposition one while in Taiwan it’s more of a capability situation. So in that way, I think they’re fundamentally different. And what’s happening with Iran and Venezuela doesn’t necessarily change that calculus. There’d still be a lot of people dead and many people question whether the United States and South Korea, at least the United States, South Korea, it’s an existential threat. So whether the United States would be willing to continue fighting a war of that size. So I don’t think the parallel’s quite as dark as it is with how it impacts U.S. capabilities in the Taiwan situation.DAN KURTZ-PHELANAnd the growing confidence with which the North Koreans could put a major American city and the continent of the United States at risk does not change the outcome of those war games meaningfully?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo this is why the number one thing, and I think what has saved Kim the past couple of years, is that they have not successfully tested a three-stage ICBM that credibly could reach the United States. I think that would be a game changer if the United States felt like that nuclear threat extended to the continental United States. Now, because we have this extended deterrence in which we say to our allies and partners, “Listen, you don’t need nuclear weapons because if nuclear weapons are used against you, we will respond with a nuclear weapon.” Our allies and partners, when we say, “Oh, we feel more safe because we’re not under threat.” Obviously Japan is, obviously the South Koreans are. And I’d like to reassure them that actually I think it’s more likely that the United States would use nuclear weapons against North Korea in a response given that they cannot touch us.So you shouldn’t feel kind of insecure about the fact that the United States, oh, the United States is safe over there, so they’re not going to get involved. I think it’s actually because we’re safe that we’re more likely to execute on the extended deterrence promises that we’ve had. Now, if Kim starts testing again and goes that direction, I think it is a game changer if he successfully tests that capability. So my priority would not even be denuclearization, but in some sort of agreement about the status would be mainly about delivery systems, that he never develops the capability to deliver those nuclear weapons beyond the region. I think that would be a fundamental issue for the nation.DAN KURTZ-PHELANI’m struck by your confidence that he couldn’t put any targets in the continental United States at risk. That doesn’t seem to be the consensus among North Korea watchers at this point.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo there’s two things. Deterrence is about perception. And to be able to deter the United States, you have to show a credible . . . that people in the United States think that you can do this, and it’s never been successfully tested. And so while you might be like, oh, well, they’re on their way, and maybe if they tried it, it could, I don’t think you have the deterrent until there’s a series of successful tests that says, yes, I have this reliable, incredible capability. And then they start fielding significant numbers of ICBMs in that the United States does have defenses. We have the regional three-tiered missile defense against North Korea. And then of course we have defenses in the United States. And so it can be frustrating. A lot of times what deterrence is is we say to a country like, “You should be afraid of this or you should care about these things.” And they’re like, “Well, we’re not and we don’t.” And so that means that deterrence doesn’t hold.And so I think the capability is one thing, perceptions are the other. So I’m not going to say that 100 percent if they put a nuclear warhead on that missile, that it could not reach the United States. I’m just saying that they have not demonstrated the capability that they can do that yet. And so until that happens, the strategic environment is such that the United States can continue under the assumption that they can’t.DAN KURTZ-PHELANIs there anything that the United States could conceivably do in the next five years or could plausibly do in the next five years that would change the deterrence calculation in the Taiwan Strait to focus on that riskier example? What would you be stressing?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROThe thing about the Taiwan Strait that’s very frustrating, as much as I talk about the likelihood of Chinese use of force is the way that I read it, I read it the way that a lot of my critics do, which is Xi Jinping is an extremely cautious leader that doesn’t want to pay significant costs for the unification with Taiwan. We split because I see that the nature of the operational environment is that those costs don’t exist. And that’s the main problem. If the United States had the capability to in a timely manner sink the majority of an invading force, China wouldn’t consider doing this.Now, what would we need to demonstrate that? Land-based intermediate range ballistic missiles, number one. You have the mass. They’re far enough away out of the Chinese threat ring, so they can’t be blown up. We don’t have that capability. We were in a treaty until 2017 that made it so we couldn’t develop it. Now we’ve been out of that treaty for nine years. So I’m always like, where are these capabilities? And there’s a lot of reasons I’m given why we don’t have them, but that, I think, would fundamentally solve the problem.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWhat are the reasons you’re given?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo I think part of it is that the way the technology is developed, the services are relying more on pathways that they know. And that’s why you’ve probably heard of development of drones or unmanned systems. This is going to be the way to sink that invading force over Taiwan. My own view is that that’s just a way to lose the war more cheaply because unmanned systems don’t solve any of the problems that I have spoken about when it comes to projecting power over the Taiwan Strait. The air defenses over the Taiwan Strait are so robust that a U.S. aircraft can’t get in there without being shot down. What is the difference for a drone? None. The only difference is maybe we can produce more of them so some of them can get through and they’re not more capable of carrying extra munitions to bring more mass to target.But then the fundamental issue is where are you going to put them? Because access basing and overflight is extremely limited. That’s the number one issue in the Indo-Pacific. So you’re telling me you’re going to build thousands of drones. Where are you going to put them? And I will just say that anyone who thinks that you are putting them anywhere near Taiwan, you’re crazy because our agreement with China is that there are no military installations on the island of Taiwan. I think things like putting U.S. troops or things like that on the island of Taiwan, declarations of independence, saying the U.S. nuclear deterrent extends to Taiwan, these are 100 percent a war starts the next day type of scenario. So I don’t think that’s something that we want to risk.If the Japanese fought alongside the United States from day one, not just logistical support, not just like once you guys get going, we’ll support you. But they were willing to day one start the fighting even if the United States isn’t there en masse, that would be enough to deter China because they can bring that mass into theater in two days. It takes our submarines three weeks, it takes theirs two to three days. That would be a game changer.DAN KURTZ-PHELANSorry to interrupt you, but I suppose that explains the ferocity of the Chinese response when Prime Minister Takaichi made statements about Japanese interests in Taiwan that had gone beyond what her predecessor said.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTRORight. I mean any Japanese involvement in the early stages in an offensive way in that conflict would be really difficult for the Chinese to deal with. So that’s more of a political move. Things like more access basing and overflight, that’s what the Biden administration was working on. The new bases in the Philippines, new points of access in the southwest Islands of Japan, just so you can bring more closer in to respond quickly. But the United States hasn’t done a lot, at least in terms of air power to build up those bases in the Philippines. Why? Because people say they’re not survivable. Why should we put money into these countries when for the Philippines we don’t know if they’ll let us use it? And two, the Chinese can blow it up. My view is if we don’t have more stuff close in, the war is lost.And so either if we build up the Philippines and we are about to fight World War III with China and the government in the Philippines says we don’t want you using your bases. My view is we’re going to use them. I mean, I’m sorry, that’s what’s going to happen. So I think the political reliability is irrelevant. And when it comes to survivability, the whole Chinese strategy is to not involve the United States. So a lot of these concerns that prevent us from building the force posture we need in the Indo-Pacific says, listen, on day one, China is going to blow up all of our bases in the Philippines and Japan. And that just doesn’t make any sense.First of all, I’ve never seen it from the Chinese side when you read about our campaigns over Taiwan, that day one they do that, because then the United States is all in. Even if they take over Taiwan in two and a half weeks and we haven’t brought the mass, they’ve killed thousands of Americans. Once we show up, even if it is a month or two later, we’re showing up.So I kind of think about it like there’s going to be a sanctuary period of a couple of weeks. And if China assesses, listen, the United States has successfully prevented us from taking Taiwan. They try to take Taiwan. The United States is close enough with enough mass that we prevent them from landing. Then they have a decision to make. Do they go home or do they escalate and attack U.S. bases? And at that point, maybe the United States can pull forces farther away, but it’s not going to happen the first couple of weeks.So I think a lot of it has to do with there’s different conflicts here that you can plan for a short war. It’s not the case that if you can fight a three-year war against China and win, you can fight a 30-day war and win. I think people get that confused and think, oh, if I plan for the worst case scenario, it means I’m prepared for everything short of that, but that’s not how military operations work.DAN KURTZ-PHELANThe other striking response to Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi’s statements about Taiwan came from the Trump administration. There was not a lot of support, rhetorical or otherwise, when China came after her and came after Japanese interests in a variety of ways. It has caused some to question the health of U.S. alliances emerging from Trump’s second term. Where do you think the key U.S. alliances in Asia will be at that point?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROI think the initial reaction of many when they see U.S. allies and partners, Australia and Japan and South Korea coming closer together is like, this is a deterrent. This is a good thing. These countries are voicing opinions about the security environment that maybe the Biden administration had hoped that they would’ve voiced a couple of years ago. So it seems like a win. But I worry that it’s a sign of the atrophy of the alliance system such that the deterrence is weakened. If these leaders are going out and trying to make these public statements, they don’t get the support from the United States. So China sees the wedge.China’s number one strategic goal, vis-à-vis the alliances, has been to break them up. So if they had to choose between, oh, well, South Korea and Japan are closer, but they’re completely alienated from the United States. I mean, that’s a better situation for them than what existed before, which is the United States had strong relations with each of them individually, but they didn’t coordinate. So I do worry that this is how the Chinese are going to see it. That yes, the push in capitals in Asia to be more active on these security issues is a sign that their relationship with the United States is weakened.DAN KURTZ-PHELANI was struck when Phil Gordon, a senior Biden administration official was on the podcast a couple of months ago. He talked about deterrence as being a combination of trust and capabilities. So you could imagine capabilities going up quite a bit, but it depends on how you weigh it against that decline in trust. And I suppose that’ll be a question that we’ll be exploring for several years.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROLet me just also say though, I mean, capabilities going up. If you take out the aggregation with U.S. capabilities, yes, maybe Japan’s capabilities go up, Australia’s capabilities go up. But if you take out the U.S. capabilities from that aggregation, you are in a far worse capabilities position than you were if those countries had done nothing but the United States was committed to the region.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWe started the conversation by talking about Beijing’s strategy for this moment and the, as you see, a relative continuity with strategy as the Chinese have thought of it for several years. Do you think its strategy is working well? Does it have a good strategy for this moment of rupture or inflection point or whatever we call it?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROSo I think it’s a bit too early to say 100 percent it’s working well. The reason I say that is less one of an analytical conclusion and more one of wishful thinking, which is the United States tends to get its act together at the last possible minute. We are close to that last possible minute, but we’re not there yet.So China’s strategy has worked so far. The United States has recognized the challenge, and for a couple of years now we’ve tried to come up with responses to the challenge. The United States is in a very strong position. We still have the largest economy in the world. We’re energy independent. We tend to get in our own way, but if we wanted to, I still believe that we could get our act together in time to strengthen the international order, be leaders of this international system, strengthen deterrence in the Indo-Pacific and the rest of the world.And if all of a sudden there is that pushback because of how strong China has become and because of how assertive they’ve become, there is more of a wake-up call, like a Sputnik moment at some point in the United States that’s going to be very problematic for China. And that’s why as I wrote in “The Stealth Superpower,” a big part of their strategy was to build power in ways that would not lead to that Sputnik moment. Or from their perspective, at the very least, when the United States really realized that they had to do something, it would be too late. So it’s too soon to say that it’s too late. I still think there’s some runway here for the United States to do the right thing.I also just want to throw out the theoretical point that U.S.-China competition doesn’t have to be a competition to the bottom. There is a world in which we are competing to be the best partners to countries in the developing world, to be the best supporters of development in the developing world, to be the best in terms of environmental protection or green energy. In terms of what we compete on, there is a world in which we are both better countries because we have to compete with the other side. We’re not in that world right now, but it’s possible that we could get there with the right leaders, right strategy. So I haven’t given up hope yet that you look at 250 years of U.S. strategy. I would say that we’re constantly in turmoil at home, but that doesn’t necessarily mean we don’t do the right thing abroad.DAN KURTZ-PHELANSo it’s the stealth superpower versus the procrastinating superpower. That’s the competition we have.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTRORight, exactly. And maybe it’s like the tortoise and the hare. And the hare, we’re kind of taking our nap, but maybe there’s an alternative version in which we wake up right in time to beat the tortoise.DAN KURTZ-PHELANBut the tortoise wins that race is my memory of the parable, right?ORIANA SKYLAR MASTRONot only does the tortoise win that race, but if you look at every rising power that is in Graham Ellison’s book of rising powers across history, over 80 percent of the time they overtake the great power, either through war or peacefully. He focuses mainly on whether there’s a war. But I recoded them just out of curiosity, and 80 percent of the time it does happen that the rising power, that the tortoise wins. But American exceptionalism, maybe this is that 20 percent.DAN KURTZ-PHELANWe will be the hare that wins. Oriana, thank you for the great pieces you’ve done for Foreign Affairs over the years. I’m sure there’ll be more to come in coming months and years, and thanks again for doing this today.ORIANA SKYLAR MASTROYeah, thank you for having me.
